_purpose: Life Pattern for the company town lifecycle — mining, lumber, and mill towns built around a single extractive industry, 1870-1950. The arc from boom through stability to bust that shaped millions of families in Appalachia, the Great Lakes, and the industrial periphery.
short_description: A mining, lumber, or mill company builds a town around its operation — company houses, company store, company school, company church. Workers are paid in scrip, live in company housing, and shop at the company store. The town exists only because the resource exists. When the resource depletes or the market crashes, the town dies. This pattern describes what happened to families who lived inside a closed economic system for one to three generations, from recruitment and arrival through community formation and labor conflict to resource depletion and population collapse.
regions: Southern West Virginia coalfields (McDowell, Mingo, Logan, Raleigh, Fayette counties)
Northeastern Pennsylvania anthracite region (Schuylkill, Luzerne, Lackawanna, Carbon counties)
Eastern Kentucky coalfields (Harlan, Perry, Letcher, Pike counties)
Alabama iron and coal district (Birmingham hinterland)
Great Lakes iron and copper ranges (Michigan, Minnesota)
Southern Appalachian lumber camps (North Carolina, Tennessee)
Southern textile mill villages (Piedmont Carolinas, Georgia, Alabama)
population: Multiethnic and multiracial labor force recruited by company agents: local Appalachian families displaced from subsistence farms, African American families recruited from the Deep South, and Southern and Eastern European immigrants — Italian, Hungarian, Polish, Slovak, Lithuanian, Welsh. The family was the household unit, though many camps began as all-male operations with boarding houses. Women's labor — cooking for boarders, maintaining the household, managing scrip — was essential but unpaid. Children entered the breaker, the tipple, or the mill floor as early as age eight.

lifecycle_phases.description: A company acquires mineral rights or timber leases in a remote location — typically a mountain hollow, a lakeside ridge, or a riverbank site with no prior settlement. Construction begins: the mine or mill, a rail spur to connect it to markets, and the town itself. Houses are built in rows — identical frame structures, often four rooms, on company-owned land. A company store is erected. A school, a church, and sometimes a doctor's office follow. Labor agents fan out — to Alabama cotton fields, to Carpathian villages, to played-out farms in the surrounding mountains — offering wages, housing, and transportation. The first wave of workers arrives, often single men who live in boarding houses until they can send for families. Within months, a hollow that held nothing but trees contains a functioning town of several hundred people.
record_signatures: Company incorporation records and mineral-lease documents
Railroad construction and spur-line permits
Labor recruitment advertisements in European and Southern newspapers
Ship manifests listing company-town destinations
Early census records showing sudden population appearance in previously empty districts
lifecycle_phases.narrative_hook: In {year}, the {company_name} company drove a rail spur into a hollow in {origin_county} that had no name on any map, and within a year, {ancestor_name} was living in a four-room company house with a number instead of an address.

lifecycle_phases.phase_name: Stability: Community Formation and Institutional Maturity
lifecycle_phases.description: The camp becomes a town. Families replace single men. Ethnic churches are built — a Polish Catholic church, a Welsh Methodist chapel, a Black Baptist congregation, sometimes all within a quarter mile. Fraternal lodges organize by nationality. The company store becomes the social center. Children attend the company school. A second generation is born that knows no other life. The work is dangerous — mine explosions, roof falls, saw-blade accidents, brown lung — and funerals are frequent. Labor conflict intensifies as workers organize against the company's total control: the Paint Creek-Cabin Creek strike (1912-1913), the Matewan massacre (1920), the Battle of Blair Mountain (1921). The company responds with Baldwin-Felts agents, evictions, and blacklists. Through it all, daily life continues — gardens are planted behind company houses, baseball teams form, children play in the slag heaps, and women maintain community networks that the company neither controls nor acknowledges.
record_signatures: Company employment records listing race, nationality, and job classification
Company store ledger books documenting scrip transactions
Ethnic church parish registers (baptisms, marriages, burials)
Mine inspection reports listing accidents and fatalities
Census records showing extraordinary ethnic and racial diversity in tiny communities
Fraternal lodge membership records
lifecycle_phases.narrative_hook: By {decade}, the camp had a Polish church, a Black church, a company school, and a baseball team — everything a town needed except the one thing the company would never provide: the deed to any of it.

lifecycle_phases.phase_name: Decline: Resource Depletion and Labor Upheaval
lifecycle_phases.description: The resource begins to thin. Coal seams narrow. Timber stands are clear-cut. Ore deposits play out. The company reduces shifts, lays off workers, or introduces machinery that replaces hand labor. Mechanization hits the coal industry especially hard — a continuous mining machine does the work of dozens of men. Simultaneously, the Depression crushes commodity prices: coal that sold for four dollars a ton in 1920 sells for two dollars in 1933. The company cuts wages, which provokes strikes, which produce evictions. Families thrown out of company houses live in tent colonies through Appalachian winters. Some mines close permanently. The population begins to leave — young men first, then families. The company store closes. The school consolidates with a distant county system. The church loses its congregation.
record_signatures: Mine closure and bankruptcy records
Union membership rolls and strike records
County relief rolls and WPA employment records
Census records showing population decline between 1930 and 1940
Company eviction notices and court records
lifecycle_phases.narrative_hook: When the {company_name} mine cut to three days a week in {year}, {ancestor_name} likely understood what the company would never say: the coal that built this town was running out, and when it was gone, the town would go with it.

lifecycle_phases.phase_name: Bust: Closure, Ghost Town, and Diaspora
lifecycle_phases.description: The mine closes, the mill shuts down, or the timber company pulls out. The company sells the houses — if anyone will buy them — or simply abandons them. Families scatter: to Akron and Detroit for factory work, to Baltimore and Washington for service jobs, to any relative in any city who can provide a floor to sleep on. The town that existed only because of the resource ceases to exist as a community. Within a generation, the company houses rot or are dismantled. The rail spur is pulled up. The hollow returns to woods. What remains are cemetery headstones — many in foreign languages — marking the lives of people who built a community that was designed from the beginning to be temporary. The families carry the company-town experience forward: a deep distrust of employers, a fierce loyalty to kin networks, and a practical knowledge of how to survive on very little.
record_signatures: Company dissolution and property-sale records
Destination city directories showing former company-town residents
Social Security applications (from 1936) documenting birthplace in vanished communities
Cemetery records — often the last physical evidence of the community
County property records showing bulk sales of company-owned houses
lifecycle_phases.narrative_hook: The town where {ancestor_name} was born no longer exists on any map — the company pulled the rail spur, the houses fell in, and the hollow went back to woods, leaving nothing but a cemetery with headstones in three languages to prove anyone had ever lived there.

entry_conditions.description: Local Appalachian families whose subsistence farms can no longer support a growing population. Land has been subdivided across generations until individual plots are too small to sustain a family. The mine or mill offers cash wages — the first regular income many of these families have ever seen. This is the most common entry path for white Appalachian workers.

African American families recruited from the Deep South by company labor agents. Coal companies in particular sent agents to Alabama, Mississippi, and Georgia, promising wages, housing, and a degree of racial equality not available in the cotton economy. Many Black families entering company towns were escaping sharecropping, racial violence, or both.

Southern and Eastern European immigrants recruited by company labor agents who traveled to villages in Hungary, Italy, Poland, Slovakia, Lithuania, and Ukraine. These men typically arrived alone, lived in boarding houses, and sent for wives and families after establishing themselves. Many were sojourners who intended to earn and return — some did, but most stayed.

Workers who arrived building the rail spur that connected the mine or mill to the main line. When construction ended, some men transitioned from railroad labor to mine or mill work, staying in the community the railroad had made possible.

exit_conditions.description: The mine or mill closes permanently, eliminating all employment. Families scatter to industrial cities — Akron, Detroit, Cleveland, Baltimore, Cincinnati — wherever relatives or ethnic networks offer a foothold. This exit is involuntary; the town is dying around them.

Mechanization reduces the labor force before the resource is fully depleted. Continuous mining machines, mechanical loaders, and automated mill equipment eliminate hand-labor jobs. Younger workers leave for factory cities; older workers try to hold on until retirement or the final closure.

Black families in company towns — particularly in the West Virginia and Alabama coalfields — leave for Northern cities as part of the Great Migration. They face the same push factors as white families (mine closure, mechanization) compounded by racial discrimination in an industry that was relatively integrated underground but segregated above ground.

The children of company-town workers use education, military service, or marriage to leave before the bust phase. A miner who spent thirty years underground is determined that his son will not follow him into the mine. This exit is planned, deliberate, and often sacrificial — the family foregoes the child's labor contribution so the child can attend school.

chain_examples: scots_irish_frontier → yeoman_farming_stability → appalachian_subsistence_squeeze → company_town_lifecycle → appalachian_out_migration → depression_survival_strategies

daily_life.narrative: Life in a company town was defined by the mine whistle. It blew at dawn to start the shift, at midday for the brief meal break, and at quitting time — or at any hour to signal a disaster underground. A miner's wife rose before the whistle to pack his dinner bucket with biscuits, beans, and water. She spent the day hauling water from the company pump, heating it for laundry on a wood stove, tending the garden behind the company house, and — if the family took in boarders — cooking and cleaning for additional men. Children walked to the company school or, if old enough, went to the breaker or the tipple. Saturday was payday at the company store, where scrip was redeemed for groceries at prices the company set. Sunday was church. The rhythm was constant, the horizon was the ridgeline, and the danger was always present.

details.item: The company store and scrip system
details.description: Most company towns paid wages partly or entirely in scrip — metal tokens or paper certificates redeemable only at the company store. Prices at the company store ran 20 to 40 percent above market rates in nearby independent towns. A miner earning $3.00 per day in scrip might find that $2.40 of it went to rent, store charges, doctor fees, and other company deductions before he saw any value. The 1913 U.S. Commission on Industrial Relations found that company stores were the most common source of worker grievance in the coalfields, not because of price alone but because the system eliminated the possibility of saving or shopping elsewhere.
details.source: Corbin, David Alan. 'Life, Work, and Rebellion in the Coal Fields: The Southern West Virginia Miners, 1880-1922.' University of Illinois Press, 1981, pp. 10-28.

details.description: Company houses were typically identical frame structures — four rooms, no plumbing, no electricity (until the 1920s in some camps), set on posts without foundations. Rent was deducted from wages at $2 to $6 per month. A family of eight occupied the same four rooms as a family of three. Outhouses stood behind the houses. Water came from a company pump or a creek. Gardens were essential — nearly every family grew beans, corn, potatoes, and tomatoes in the narrow strip of ground between the house and the hillside. The sameness of the housing is visible in photographs: rows of identical white or unpainted houses climbing a hillside, with the tipple looming at the head of the hollow.
details.source: Eller, Ronald D. 'Miners, Millhands, and Mountaineers: Industrialization of the Appalachian South, 1880-1930.' University of Tennessee Press, 1982, pp. 165-185.

details.description: Coal mining in this era was extraordinarily dangerous. Between 1900 and 1930, an average of 2,000 miners were killed annually in the United States — roughly six per day. Major disasters (Monongah, 1907: 362 dead; Dawson, 1913: 263 dead; Benwood, 1924: 119 dead) punctuated a steady toll of individual roof falls, gas explosions, and equipment accidents. Every family in a coal camp knew someone who had been killed or permanently disabled. Mine-accident death records, where they survive, list name, nationality, age, cause of death, and surviving dependents — making them genealogically valuable despite their grim content.
details.source: Fishback, Price V. 'Soft Coal, Hard Choices: The Economic Welfare of Bituminous Coal Miners, 1890-1930.' Oxford University Press, 1992, pp. 74-95.

details.description: Women in company towns built the social infrastructure the company did not provide. They organized church auxiliaries, burial societies, and mutual-aid networks. When a miner was killed, neighboring women brought food, sat with the widow, and pooled resources to help the family survive until the next arrangement could be made. During strikes, women ran soup kitchens, walked picket lines, and maintained community solidarity against company eviction threats. Their labor was invisible in company records but essential to community survival. As one West Virginia miner's wife told an interviewer: 'The company owned everything but us — and they tried to own us too, but the women wouldn't let them.'
details.source: Hall, Jacquelyn Dowd, et al. 'Like a Family: The Making of a Southern Cotton Mill World.' University of North Carolina Press, 1987, pp. 120-155.

economic_trajectory.narrative: The economic trajectory of a company-town family was volatile — dependent entirely on the price of the commodity the town existed to produce. When coal or lumber prices were high, the mine ran full shifts and families had steady income, even if much of it circulated back to the company through the store. When prices dropped, shifts were cut to three or two days a week, and families survived on gardens, credit at the company store, and mutual aid. The trajectory was neither accumulating nor steadily declining but boom-bust-boom-bust, with each bust eroding the family's reserves a little further. Over a generation, most company-town families ended where they started — owning nothing, dependent on wages they could not control, living in houses they could be evicted from at the company's discretion.

details.description: A coal miner in southern West Virginia earned roughly $2.50 to $4.00 per day in the 1910s and 1920s — good wages by rural Appalachian standards but subject to enormous variation. Miners were paid by the ton of coal loaded, not by the hour, meaning income depended on the thickness of the seam, the availability of cars, and the company's weighing practices. After deductions for rent ($2-6/month), company-store charges, doctor fees ($1-2/month per family), burial fund, and school tax, a miner's take-home pay might be half his gross earnings. During slack periods, when the mine ran only two or three days a week, families fell behind on company-store accounts and carried balances forward — a debt cycle uncomfortably similar to the sharecropping system.
details.source: Fishback, Price V. 'Soft Coal, Hard Choices,' pp. 40-65.

details.item: Property ownership — or the lack of it
details.description: The defining economic feature of the company town was the absence of property ownership among workers. The company owned the land, the houses, the store, and often the church and school buildings. A family that lived in a company town for thirty years accumulated no real property and built no equity. When the mine closed, they left with what they could carry. Census records from company towns show occupation after occupation listed as 'miner' or 'laborer' with no property value recorded. This absence of accumulated wealth distinguishes company-town families from their contemporaries in independent towns, where even modest homeownership built intergenerational equity.
details.source: Eller, Ronald D. 'Miners, Millhands, and Mountaineers,' pp. 190-210.

details.description: The Depression hit company towns with devastating force. Coal production in West Virginia fell by 40 percent between 1929 and 1933. Lumber production collapsed. Hundreds of mines and mills closed permanently. Families that had survived on volatile but present wages suddenly had no income at all. The company store extended credit until it could not. County relief systems, designed for small rural populations, were overwhelmed by thousands of newly destitute families. In Harlan County, Kentucky, the Red Cross reported widespread malnutrition among mining families by 1931. The New Deal brought some relief — WPA jobs, surplus commodities, CCC enrollment for young men — but the underlying economy of many company towns never recovered.
details.source: Hevener, John W. 'Which Side Are You On? The Harlan County Coal Miners, 1931-1939.' University of Illinois Press, 1978, pp. 1-30.

family_dynamics.narrative: Company-town families were shaped by isolation, danger, and the closed economy. Large families were common — both from cultural norms and from the economic logic of more earners per household. Gender roles were sharply defined: men worked underground or in the mill, women maintained the household and the community's social fabric. The constant possibility of the breadwinner's death or disability shaped family planning, savings behavior, and the intensity of burial-society membership. Ethnic intermarriage across the European immigrant groups was common by the second generation — a Slovak miner's daughter might marry an Italian miner's son — though racial intermarriage remained rare. The second generation faced a stark choice: follow the father into the mine or leave. Many families invested everything in one child's education as a planned exit from the cycle.

details.item: Child labor and early entry to work
details.description: In coal towns, boys entered the breaker — the building where coal was sorted and slate picked out by hand — as young as eight or nine. By fourteen or sixteen, they went underground. In mill towns, children entered the factory floor at similar ages. The 1900 census shows thousands of children aged 10-15 listed as 'breaker boy,' 'trapper,' or 'mill hand' in company-town districts. Child labor laws, where they existed, were weakly enforced in remote company towns. A family's economic survival often depended on children's wages, creating a tension between the desire for education and the need for income that many families resolved by sacrificing one child's labor for another child's schooling.
details.source: Corbin, David Alan. 'Life, Work, and Rebellion in the Coal Fields,' pp. 30-45.

details.description: Before a miner could afford to send for his family — or if he was unmarried — he boarded with an established family. A company house designed for one family might contain the host family plus three to six boarders. The boarding wife cooked, cleaned, and did laundry for all of them, earning income that supplemented her husband's wages. Census records from company towns frequently show 'boarder' and 'lodger' entries alongside family members. The boarding relationship created quasi-familial bonds — boarders became godparents, witnesses at weddings, and members of the household's social network. For immigrant men, the boarding house was the point of cultural entry, where they learned English, local customs, and survival strategies from compatriots who had arrived earlier.
details.source: Bodnar, John. 'The Transplanted: A History of Immigrants in Urban America.' Indiana University Press, 1985, pp. 71-84.

details.description: Company towns, particularly in the coalfields, were among the most ethnically diverse communities in rural America. A single camp might contain families from ten or more nationalities, plus African American families from the Deep South and local Appalachian families. The first generation maintained strict ethnic boundaries — separate churches, separate lodges, separate boarding houses. By the second generation, these boundaries softened. Census records from the 1920s and 1930s show increasing rates of interethnic marriage: a Polish father, an Italian mother; a Hungarian father, a Slovak mother. Racial boundaries were more durable, but the underground workplace created bonds of mutual dependence across racial lines that were unusual for the era.
details.source: Trotter, Joe William, Jr. 'Coal, Class, and Color: Blacks in Southern West Virginia, 1915-32.' University of Illinois Press, 1990, pp. 50-75.

record_signatures.description: Mine and mill companies maintained detailed employment records listing workers by name, nationality or race, job classification, hire date, and sometimes family size. These records survive unevenly — some are in corporate archives or university special collections (West Virginia University, University of Pittsburgh), while many were destroyed when companies dissolved. Where they survive, they are often the most complete documentation of a worker's career.
record_signatures.genealogical_value: Establishes employment dates, job progression, and family connection to a specific company and location. Nationality and race fields can confirm immigrant origins. Pay records document economic condition with precision.

record_signatures.description: Account books recording every transaction at the company store — purchases of food, clothing, tools, and household goods, charged against wages in scrip. Where they survive, they document daily purchases by family name over months or years.
record_signatures.genealogical_value: Provides month-by-month evidence of family presence. Purchase patterns reveal family size, dietary habits, and economic condition. The ledger may be the only record showing a family's presence between census years.

record_signatures.description: State mine inspection reports, required by law in most coal-producing states by 1900, document working conditions, safety violations, and accidents. Fatal-accident reports list the deceased worker's name, age, nationality, cause of death, and surviving dependents. Annual inspection reports list every mine in the state with employment figures.
record_signatures.genealogical_value: Death records for mine fatalities include family information not available elsewhere. Inspection reports confirm which mines were operating in which years, helping to locate families in specific communities.

record_signatures.description: Catholic, Orthodox, and Protestant churches organized by nationality maintained baptismal, marriage, and burial records in the language of the congregation — Polish, Slovak, Lithuanian, Italian, Hungarian, Welsh. These parishes were often the most durable institution in a company town, outlasting the company itself.
record_signatures.genealogical_value: Documents vital events (births, marriages, deaths) that may not appear in civil records, especially for immigrant families. Records are often in the original language, providing surnames and place-of-origin information in their native spelling. Godparent and witness entries reveal kinship and community networks.

record_signatures.description: Federal census records from company-town districts show entire communities organized around a single industry. The 1900-1940 censuses are particularly valuable, recording occupation, birthplace, parents' birthplace, language spoken, home ownership status (invariably 'rented' in company towns), and literacy. Enumerators typically walked through company towns house by house, producing a complete snapshot of the community.
record_signatures.genealogical_value: Reveals the full community context — neighbors, boarding relationships, ethnic composition, and occupational structure. Comparing consecutive censuses shows which families stayed and which left, mapping the lifecycle of the community itself.

cultural_markers.description: Family stories mentioning 'scrip,' 'the company store,' 'the commissary,' or 'drawing against your pay' indicate company-town origins. The phrase 'I owe my soul to the company store' — popularized by Merle Travis and later Tennessee Ernie Ford — originated in the coalfields and was not metaphorical.

cultural_markers.marker: Mining-related surnames in foreign languages on cemetery headstones
cultural_markers.description: Company-town cemeteries frequently contain headstones inscribed in Polish, Slovak, Italian, Hungarian, Lithuanian, and Welsh alongside English-language markers. The presence of multiple foreign-language headstones in a rural Appalachian or Great Lakes cemetery is strong evidence of a company-town community that has since disappeared.

Death certificates listing 'miner's asthma,' 'black lung,' 'silicosis,' or mine-accident causes for multiple family members across generations indicate company-town occupational patterns. A family in which the grandfather, father, and uncle all died of respiratory disease before age sixty was almost certainly a mining family.

cultural_markers.marker: Absence from property records despite decades of residence
cultural_markers.description: A family that appears in the same census district for twenty or thirty years without ever appearing in county deed or tax records was likely living in company housing. This absence of property records — combined with consistent mining or milling occupations — is a documentary signature of the company-town system.

narrative_hooks: The company built the town, and the town existed for the company — {ancestor_name} lived in a house with a number instead of an address, bought groceries with scrip instead of cash, and worked in a mine that owned everything above ground as surely as it hollowed out everything below.

In {origin_county}, the company owned the houses, the store, the school, and the doctor — and by the time rent and store charges were deducted from {ancestor_name}'s pay, the wages that had seemed so promising were barely enough to keep the family fed.

Three languages were spoken on {ancestor_name}'s street — and all three families went underground every morning into the same darkness, because the mine did not care where a man was born, only that he could swing a pick.

When the {company_name} mine closed in {year}, {ancestor_name}'s family lost not just a job but a home, a school, a church, and a community — everything the company had built could be taken away when the coal ran out.

The cemetery is all that remains of the town where {ancestor_name} was born — headstones in Polish and English marking the lives of families who built a community in a hollow that the map has since forgotten.

{ancestor_name} was probably paid in scrip — company currency worth its face value at the company store and nothing at all anywhere else — which meant that the wages the company paid came back to the company before they ever left town.

counter_narratives.myth: Company towns were benevolent institutions — the company provided housing, a store, medical care, and schools that workers could not have obtained otherwise.
counter_narratives.reality: The company provided these services not from benevolence but from necessity (there was nothing else in a remote hollow) and from the economic logic of control. Company housing tied workers to the job — eviction followed termination or union activity. Company stores captured wages through scrip and above-market pricing. Company doctors were often inadequate and always loyal to the company. The 'benevolence' was a system of dependency that prevented workers from accumulating independence.
counter_narratives.source: Corbin, David Alan. 'Life, Work, and Rebellion in the Coal Fields,' pp. 10-28.

counter_narratives.myth: Coal-camp communities were places of unrelenting misery with no real social life or community bonds.
counter_narratives.reality: Company towns generated vibrant, resilient communities despite the economic constraints. Ethnic churches, fraternal lodges, baseball teams, garden clubs, and women's mutual-aid networks created rich social lives. The multiethnic character of many camps produced cultural exchange — Italian, Slovak, and Appalachian food traditions blended; children learned phrases in each other's languages. The solidarity forged underground and during strikes created bonds that families carried for generations. Many former company-town residents remembered their communities with genuine affection, even as they acknowledged the exploitation.
counter_narratives.source: Trotter, Joe William, Jr. 'Coal, Class, and Color,' pp. 75-100.

counter_narratives.myth: Company towns in the coalfields were racially segregated in the same way as the Jim Crow South.
counter_narratives.reality: The racial dynamics of company towns were more complicated than simple segregation. Underground, work crews were often integrated — Black and white miners worked side by side in conditions where mutual dependence overrode racial hostility. Above ground, housing was typically segregated by section (not by camp), and social institutions (churches, lodges) were separate. But the degree of day-to-day interracial contact in company towns, particularly in West Virginia, was unusual for the era. The United Mine Workers union organized across racial lines as a matter of policy (though not without tension). This is not to romanticize race relations in company towns — discrimination was real — but to note that the pattern was distinct from the plantation model of the Deep South.
counter_narratives.source: Trotter, Joe William, Jr. 'Coal, Class, and Color,' pp. 25-50.

source_references: Corbin, David Alan. 'Life, Work, and Rebellion in the Coal Fields: The Southern West Virginia Miners, 1880-1922.' University of Illinois Press, 1981.
Eller, Ronald D. 'Miners, Millhands, and Mountaineers: Industrialization of the Appalachian South, 1880-1930.' University of Tennessee Press, 1982.
Fishback, Price V. 'Soft Coal, Hard Choices: The Economic Welfare of Bituminous Coal Miners, 1890-1930.' Oxford University Press, 1992.
Trotter, Joe William, Jr. 'Coal, Class, and Color: Blacks in Southern West Virginia, 1915-32.' University of Illinois Press, 1990.
Hevener, John W. 'Which Side Are You On? The Harlan County Coal Miners, 1931-1939.' University of Illinois Press, 1978.
